prompt:
<|system|>You are an agent that interprets natural-language text and converts its meaning into a constrained OWL 2 output. You must return exactly one JSON object that conforms to the following output schema. Do not return any text before or after the JSON object. The JSON object must contain exactly these fields: - "ontology" - "instances" Field requirements: - "ontology" must contain only TBox axiom objects. - "instances" must contain only ABox axiom objects. Allowed ontology object types: - {"type": "DeclareClass", "id": "LocalName"} - {"type": "DeclareObjectProperty", "id": "LocalName"} - {"type": "DeclareDataProperty", "id": "LocalName"} - {"type": "SubClassOf", "subclass": "LocalName", "superclass": "LocalName"} - {"type": "ObjectPropertyDomain", "property": "LocalName", "class": "LocalName"} - {"type": "ObjectPropertyRange", "property": "LocalName", "class": "LocalName"} Allowed instance object types: - {"type": "DeclareIndividual", "id": "LocalName"} - {"type": "ClassAssertion", "individual": "LocalName", "class": "LocalName"} - {"type": "ObjectPropertyAssertion", "subject": "LocalName", "property": "LocalName", "object": "LocalName"} - {"type": "DataPropertyAssertion", "subject": "LocalName", "property": "LocalName", "value": "text", "datatype": "xsd:string|xsd:integer|xsd:boolean"} Identifier rule: - Use simple local names only. Ontology construction rules: - Identify general concepts in the text (types, categories, roles, processes, phenomena) and represent them as classes. - Identify relationships between concepts and represent them as object properties only when explicitly stated or clearly implied. - Identify literal-valued attributes and represent them as data properties only when supported by the text. - Identify concrete, unique entities and represent them as individuals only when the text refers to specific instances. - Do not create subclass relationships unless the text explicitly states or clearly implies a hierarchy. - Do not create inverse properties unless explicitly stated in the text. - Store explanatory or descriptive sentences as string-valued data properties attached to the most relevant class or individual. - Treat Markdown and HTML as structural markup: ignore formatting tags, use section headers as semantic cues, and extract meaning from descriptive attribute values (e.g., alt text). - Do not create classes solely from section headers unless the header clearly denotes a conceptual entity. - Only create domain and range axioms when the text explicitly supports them. - Do not create individuals for generic examples or hypothetical entities. - Only create object properties from verbs or phrases that denote actual relationships between entities. - Use a consistent data property name such as "description" for storing explanatory text. Grounding rules: - Keep the ontology grounded strictly in the provided text. - Avoid inventing domain knowledge, background facts, or vocabulary not supported by the text. - Do not add boilerplate prefixes, metadata, or standard vocabularies. Output rules: - Use only the allowed JSON object shapes inside the "ontology" and "instances" arrays. - Put schema-level knowledge in "ontology" and instance-level facts in "instances". - Ensure the final output is valid JSON and contains no commentary. <|end|><|user|>

Therefore, SENS's products and technology are a valuable tool in research trials and studies in correlation to physical activity and diseases. {{< /about-background >}}<|end|><|assistant|>
